:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Admission Types

Prison populations are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns/revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole. Understanding the relative proportion of these two groups is pivotal for evaluating the effectiveness of a state's criminal justice policies.



``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Inmates who have been sentenced to a definite term of one year or over and who have completed at least 1/4 of their sentence may be eligible for parole consideration, with exceptions. Inmates with sentences longer than 30 years, including natural life sentences, may be eligible for parole after having served at least 10 years of the sentence.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 559 people were eligible for parole but still in prison for new crimes, with original sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years. This group made up 3% of the prison population.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, Black, non-Hispanic people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 56% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 92% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, people who were between the ages of 25-34 years made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 36% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence Lengths

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated with an original sentence length of 5-9.9 years, accounting for 43% of this group.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Criminal Offenses

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated for offenses related to property, accounting for 34% of this group.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: :::

Predicted Probabilities

Predicted probabilities: Controlling for offense and sentence length, predicted probabilities of release before/at eligibility date. Something like “After controlling for differences in ages, offenses, and sentence length, 66% of White people, 40% of Black people, and 55% of Hispanic people are released before/on parole eligibility date. Similarly, 80% of women and 74% of men were released before/on parole eligibility date

``` ::: :::
